core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_5 because Quill became a protected negotiation anchor from the first turn and all final proposals gave Agent_1 Quill. Agent_5 still secured its own top item, Stone, so the first-over-last gap reflects both first-speaker framing and the higher discounted value of Agent_1's top item.
why_or_counterexample: Yes, but qualified. Agent_1 used the first speaker slot to define the deal around Quill, and later agents treated Quill with Agent_1 as part of the stable core while bargaining shifted to Stone/Jewel between Agents 4 and 5. The counterexample is that Agent_5 was not shut out: it successfully kept Stone, its top item, in the final allocation.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened with Quill as the top priority and repeatedly framed a fast Quill-centered lock. Other agents accepted Quill/Pencil/Apple as anchors, while the live conflict became Stone versus Jewel between Agents 4 and 5.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking in all rounds largely mirrored the public frame: preserve Quill with Agent_1, Pencil with Agent_3, Apple with Agent_2, then handle Stone/Jewel. Agent_5 privately focused on keeping Stone, not challenging Agent_1's Quill.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 already had four of five proposals matching Option A, with Agent_1 receiving Quill and Agent_5 receiving Stone. Round 2 had one Stone/Jewel swap proposal. By Round 3, all proposals were identical and preserved Agent_1's Quill.
phase_notes.private_voting: Early failures came from self-interested rejections and some parse-error vote records, especially around Agent_2's high-utility alternative and Agent_4's Stone preference. Round 3 private votes accepted all proposals because each voter received its protected item.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections consistently diagnosed consensus failure as Stone/Jewel tension, not Quill. They recommended keeping the core anchors fixed and using follow-up rebalancing for Agents 4 and 5.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker set the Quill agenda
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first public move declared Quill as the top priority and framed the negotiation around quick coalitions for Quill. Subsequent speakers explicitly described the emerging deal as Quill-centered.

evidence.quote: My top priority: Quill. I value it at 53.
Quill-centric plan is aggressive and quick

mechanisms.theme: Quill became protected while conflict moved elsewhere
mechanisms.explanation: By later rounds, Agent_1's desired item was treated as part of the fixed core. The contested issue was not whether Agent_1 kept Quill, but how Agents 4 and 5 split Stone and Jewel.

evidence.quote: It locks Quill with its top holder (Agent_1) for speed.
The main friction remains Stone vs Jewel between Agents 4 and 5.
clear alignment on Pencil staying with Agent_3 and on Quill staying with Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Formal proposal convergence preserved Agent_1's item
mechanisms.explanation: The formal proposal process converged on the same allocation that gave Agent_1 Quill. By Round 3 every proposal matched that allocation and all received unanimous acceptance.

evidence.quote: Quill to Agent_1, Apple to Agent_2, Pencil to Agent_3

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 used its late position to correct the baseline toward Stone and ultimately kept Stone. This limits the first-position story: Agent_1's advantage was agenda protection and higher item value, not deprivation of the last speaker.

evidence.quote: Agent_5 gets Stone, which is my top item
Stone is allocated to Agent_5 (my top item, value 40)

mechanisms.theme: Private reasoning reinforced the anchored core
mechanisms.explanation: Raw private thinking and later votes show that the agents internalized a stable core around Quill, Pencil, and Apple. Agent_1 accepted even the Stone/Jewel swap because Quill stayed fixed.

evidence.quote: Quill is the pivotal asset driving quick, unanimous alignment.
This yields my top utility (Quill) with minimal disruption.
Quill to Agent_1, Apple to Agent_2, Pencil to Agent_3 remain
